summary:
Sikhism (c. 1469–present) was founded by Guru Nanak Dev Ji (1469–1539 AD) in the Punjab region of northwestern India — a new religious tradition that rejected the caste system and the mediating role of priests, emphasized direct personal relationship with the formless divine (Waheguru), promoted equality of all people regardless of caste or gender, and synthesized devotional elements from both Hinduism and Islam while considering itself distinct from both. Guru Nanak's central teachings, expressed in poetry collected in the Adi Granth (later the Guru Granth Sahib), emphasized naam simran (meditation on God's name), seva (selfless service), and honest work — practices accessible to ordinary people rather than requiring asceticism or elaborate ritual. He is said to have traveled extensively across South Asia and to the Middle East, engaging with Hindu, Muslim, Buddhist, and Jain teachers.

Sikhism developed through ten human Gurus between 1469 and 1708, each contributing to the tradition's theological, institutional, and political development. Guru Nanak established the community's spiritual foundations; Guru Angad Dev Ji standardized the Gurmukhi script in which Sikh scripture is written; Guru Amar Das Ji established the Langar (community kitchen) as a practical expression of equality; Guru Arjan Dev Ji compiled the Adi Granth and built the [[event:3507|Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple)]]; and Guru Gobind Singh Ji completed the tradition by creating the Khalsa (initiated community), compiling the final Guru Granth Sahib, and declaring it the eternal living Guru. The [[event:2486|Mughal persecutions]] transformed Sikhism's character from a primarily devotional movement into one with a strong martial tradition, eventually producing the [[event:2369|Sikh Empire]] of Ranjit Singh.
